sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient presents with a nonhealing foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, approximately 1x2 cm in size, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue, but no surrounding erythema, cellulitis, fluid collection, necrosis, odor, or bony exposure.
The patient reports minimal pain and no sensation in the foot.
The ulcer has been present for approximately one year, initially presenting as a blister that became infected and red.
The patient has been on Keflex for the past five months, but the ulcer has not improved.
The patient also reports feeling tired and has had increased drainage from the ulcer lately.
Past medical history: The patient has type 2 diabetes, which is not well-controlled, with a last A1C of 10.
The patient has been on insulin, metformin, and possibly other medications for their diabetes, but their blood sugars are still in the 200s.
Review of systems: The patient reports feeling tired and has had increased drainage from the ulcer, but denies fever, chills, or other symptoms.
Current medications: Keflex, insulin, metformin (exact dosages and frequencies not specified).
Objective:
Vital signs: Not specified.

Physical exam: The patient's right foot exam reveals a 1x2 cm circular wound on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue, but no surrounding erythema, cellulitis, fluid collection, necrosis, odor, or bony exposure.
Test Results: X-ray of the right foot shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, which is not healing due to neuropathy and poor blood supply.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; Diabetic neuropathy; Poorly controlled diabetes; Uncontrolled blood sugar.
Plan:
I prescribed Colaganase ointment to be applied to the wound once a day and covered with a sterile dressing.
I will perform a debridement of the ulcer in the office.
I will order an ABI (Ankle Brachial Index) to determine the patient's blood supply.
I will provide the patient with a surgical shoe to wear on the affected foot to reduce pressure.
I will see the patient back in two weeks for follow-up and possible further debridement.
Follow-up: I will see the patient back in two weeks for follow-up and possible further debridement.
